result.markdown:
--- title: "Watch beam search decode a peptide" subtitle: "Mass-constrained beam search and the knapsack trick that prunes the dead ends, the way InstaNovo actually does it" description: > Decoding a peptide from a spectrum isn't argmax over a vocabulary, it's a search under a hard mass budget. A runnable, step-by-step look at the precursor-mass-constrained beam search in InstaNovo, and why a precomputed knapsack chart prunes branches the mass filter alone can't. date: 2026-06-26 categories: [proteomics, de novo, deep learning, decoding, instanovo] image: pruning.png # produced by the last code cell on first render image-alt: "Residues pruned per decoding step: mass filter vs knapsack" draft: true # <- remove to publish --- > Companion to [*De novo peptide sequencing from scratch*](../de-novo-from-scratch/), > which builds the fragment-ion picture this post relies on. A sequence model gives you a probability over the next residue. The obvious thing to do is take the most probable one, repeat, and stop at the end token. For text that's fine. For a peptide it's almost never right, because a peptide isn't just *likely*, it has to *weigh the right amount*. The precursor mass is measured, and the residues you emit have to add up to it within a few parts per million. Greedy decoding ignores that constraint until it's too late. So decoding a spectrum is really a **constrained search**: find the high-probability residue string whose total mass hits the precursor. This post walks through how [InstaNovo](https://github.com/instadeepai/InstaNovo) does it, first plain beam search with a running mass budget, then the knapsack variant that prunes branches the mass filter can't see are doomed. The code below is a compact, runnable reimplementation of that logic; links to the real (batched, GPU) implementation are at the end. ## A spectrum to decode We reuse the fragment-ion machinery from the [previous post](../de-novo-from-scratch/): a peptide's mass is its residues plus a water, and its b/y ions are the running prefix/suffix masses. We'll hand the decoder the theoretical peaks of a hidden target and watch it work backwards to the sequence. ::: {#setup .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Setup: masses, fragment ions, and a target spectrum"} import bisect import numpy as np import pandas as pd import matplotlib.pyplot as plt # Constants, matching instanovo/constants.py H2O_MASS = 18.0106 PROTON = 1.007276 MASS_SCALE = 10000 RESIDUE_MASS = { "G":57.02146,"A":71.03711,"S":87.03203,"P":97.05276,"V":99.06841, "T":101.04768,"C":103.00919,"L":113.08406,"I":113.08406,"N":114.04293, "D":115.02694,"Q":128.05858,"K":128.09496,"E":129.04259,"M":131.04049, "H":137.05891,"F":147.06841,"R":156.10111,"Y":163.06333,"W":186.07931, } RESIDUES = list(RESIDUE_MASS) EOS = "$" VOCAB = RESIDUES + [EOS] IDX = {r: i for i, r in enumerate(VOCAB)} def b_ions(pep): m = [RESIDUE_MASS[a] for a in pep] return np.array([sum(m[:i]) + PROTON for i in range(1, len(pep) + 1)]) def y_ions(pep): m = [RESIDUE_MASS[a] for a in pep]; n = len(pep) return np.array([sum(m[n - j:]) + H2O_MASS + PROTON for j in range(1, n + 1)]) TARGET = "PEPTIDE" precursor_mass = sum(RESIDUE_MASS[a] for a in TARGET) + H2O_MASS peaks = np.concatenate([b_ions(TARGET), y_ions(TARGET)]) print(f"target {TARGET} precursor {precursor_mass:.4f} Da ({len(peaks)} peaks)") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` target PEPTIDE precursor 799.3600 Da (14 peaks) ``` ::: ::: ## A stand-in for the model In real InstaNovo the next-residue distribution comes from a transformer that has encoded the spectrum (the `score_candidates` method of the [`Decodable`](https://github.com/instadeepai/InstaNovo/blob/55a9a3ff7c18e2748c0ef7fc6ba3213002ad6a0f/instanovo/inference/interfaces.py) interface). To keep this post runnable without weights or a GPU, we use a tiny stand-in that scores a residue by how well the b-ion it produces lands on an observed peak. It's crude, but it's enough to make the *search* behave like the real thing. ::: {#model .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} def score_next(prefix, sharpness=12.0): """Toy scorer: log-probabilities over the vocabulary given a prefix. Real InstaNovo replaces this with a transformer; the search around it is the same.""" base = sum(RESIDUE_MASS[a] for a in prefix) logits = np.full(len(VOCAB), -50.0) for r in RESIDUES: logits[IDX[r]] = -sharpness * np.abs(peaks - (base + RESIDUE_MASS[r] + PROTON)).min() logits -= logits.max() return logits - np.log(np.exp(logits).sum()) ``` ::: ## Beam search with a mass budget The trick that makes this *de novo* decoding rather than plain text generation is a single running quantity: the **remaining mass**. It starts at `precursor_mass − H₂O` and drops by one residue mass at every step. Two rules fall out of it: 1. **Prune overshoots.** Any residue heavier than the remaining budget (beyond tolerance) can't possibly appear next, set its probability to `−∞`. 2. **Force the end when the budget is spent.** When the remaining mass is within tolerance of zero, the only legal move is the end-of-sequence token. That's exactly what InstaNovo's beam search does. The core of the mass filter, [from `beam_search.py`](https://github.com/instadeepai/InstaNovo/blob/55a9a3ff7c18e2748c0ef7fc6ba3213002ad6a0f/instanovo/inference/beam_search.py): ```python # remaining_mass starts at precursor_mass - H2O_MASS, in float64 valid_mass = remaining_mass_expanded - residue_mass_delta > -mass_target_expanded # ... (plus isotope and terminal-residue handling) ... next_token_probabilities_filtered[~valid_mass] = -float("inf") ``` and when the budget is met it forces EOS onto those beams. Everything else is ordinary beam search: keep the `beam_size` highest-log-probability partial sequences, expanding across `beam_size × vocab` candidates each step. Here it is end to end: ::: {#decoder .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} def beam_decode(beam_size=4, max_len=12, mass_tolerance=5e-5, use_knapsack=False): tol = mass_tolerance * precursor_mass # ppm budget, in Da kmasses, kchart = construct_knapsack(precursor_mass) if use_knapsack else (None, None) beams = [{"seq": [], "logp": 0.0, "rem": precursor_mass - H2O_MASS}] completed, snapshots, prune = [], [], [] for step in range(max_len): cand, mass_pruned, knap_pruned = [], 0, 0 for b in beams: if abs(b["rem"]) < tol: # budget spent -> finish completed.append(b); continue lp = score_next(b["seq"]) kset = feasible_masses(kmasses, b["rem"], tol) if use_knapsack else None for r in RESIDUES: rem = b["rem"] - RESIDUE_MASS[r] if not (rem > -tol): # (1) mass filter mass_pruned += 1; continue if use_knapsack and not (kchart[r] & kset): # (2) knapsack filter knap_pruned += 1; continue cand.append({"seq": b["seq"] + [r], "logp": b["logp"] + lp[IDX[r]], "rem": rem}) cand.sort(key=lambda c: c["logp"], reverse=True) # global top-k over beams x vocab beams = cand[:beam_size] prune.append({"step": step + 1, "mass_pruned": mass_pruned, "knapsack_pruned": knap_pruned}) for rank, b in enumerate(beams, 1): snapshots.append({"step": step + 1, "beam": rank, "sequence": "".join(b["seq"]), "log_prob": round(b["logp"], 2), "remaining_mass": round(b["rem"], 2)}) if not beams: break completed += [b for b in beams if abs(b["rem"]) < tol] completed.sort(key=lambda c: c["logp"], reverse=True) return completed, pd.DataFrame(snapshots), pd.DataFrame(prune) ``` ::: ### Watch it decode Each row is a decoding step; each column is one of the four beams. Watch the remaining mass tick down and the sequences converge: ::: {#tbl-watch .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Run plain beam search and tabulate every step"} completed, snap, prune_plain = beam_decode(use_knapsack=False) watch = snap.pivot(index="step", columns="beam", values="sequence") watch.columns = [f"beam {c}" for c in watch.columns] watch ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ```{=html}
beam 1 beam 2 beam 3 beam 4
step
1 P F V T
2 PE FD PK FN
3 PEP FDL FDI PKP
4 PEPT FDLT FDIT PKPT
5 PEPTL PEPTI FDLTP FDITP
6 PEPTLD PEPTID FDLTPE FDITPE
7 PEPTLDE PEPTIDE PEPTLDK PEPTIDK
``` ::: ::: ::: {#tbl-top .cell execution_count=5} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Top-2 completed beams: [('PEPTLDE', np.float64(-1.91)), ('PEPTIDE', np.float64(-1.91))] ``` ::: ::: Look at the top two beams: **PEPTLDE** and **PEPTIDE**, with *identical* log probabilities. That's not a bug, leucine and isoleucine have exactly the same mass (113.08406 Da) and, without immonium or satellite ions to break the tie, are genuinely indistinguishable from this spectrum. It's the canonical example of why de novo sequencing reports ambiguity, and why you want a *beam* (a ranked set), not a single greedy guess. ## The dead ends the mass filter can't see The mass filter only asks a local question: *does this one residue fit under the remaining budget?* It happily keeps a residue that fits, even when **no combination of residues** can fill the leftover mass exactly. Those branches are already doomed; the model just doesn't find out until several wasted steps later, and meanwhile they crowd good hypotheses out of the beam. A **knapsack** answers the global question ahead of time. Precompute, for every achievable total mass, which residues a peptide of that mass can *begin with*. Then a residue is only valid if, after subtracting it, the remaining mass is something real residues can still realise. InstaNovo builds this chart once with a depth-first search over *feasible* masses, [`construct_knapsack`](https://github.com/instadeepai/InstaNovo/blob/55a9a3ff7c18e2748c0ef7fc6ba3213002ad6a0f/instanovo/inference/knapsack.py): ```python # chart[mass, residue] is True iff a sequence of `mass` can start with `residue` while agenda: current_mass = agenda.pop() if current_mass in visited: continue for residue, mass in scaled_residue_masses.items(): next_mass = current_mass + mass if next_mass <= scaled_max_mass: agenda.append(next_mass) chart[next_mass, residue_indices[residue]] = True visited.add(current_mass) ``` Masses are integers (scaled by `MASS_SCALE = 10000`, i.e. 0.1 mDa resolution) so the chart is an exact lookup. At decode time, the filter checks the window of feasible masses around the remaining budget and keeps only residues that can start one of them, [`_filter_knapsack`](https://github.com/instadeepai/InstaNovo/blob/55a9a3ff7c18e2748c0ef7fc6ba3213002ad6a0f/instanovo/inference/knapsack_beam_search.py), condensed: ```python lower = ((remaining_mass - tol) * mass_scale).round().long().clamp(min=0) upper = ((remaining_mass + tol) * mass_scale).round().long().clamp(max=chart.shape[0]-1) # True for residue r if any feasible mass in [lower, upper] can start with r valid = chart[mass_indices, residue_columns].any(dim=-1) ``` The runnable equivalent, a DFS to build the set of feasible masses per starting residue, and a binary-search lookup: ::: {#knapsack .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Build the knapsack chart (DFS) and the feasible-mass lookup"} def construct_knapsack(max_mass, scale=MASS_SCALE): scaled_max = round(max_mass * scale) scaled = {r: round(RESIDUE_MASS[r] * scale) for r in RESIDUES} chart = {r: {m} for r, m in scaled.items()} # masses a seq can have if it STARTS with r agenda, visited = list(scaled.values()), set() while agenda: cur = agenda.pop() if cur in visited: continue for r, m in scaled.items(): nxt = cur + m if nxt <= scaled_max: agenda.append(nxt); chart[r].add(nxt) visited.add(cur) return np.array(sorted(visited)), {r: set(s) for r, s in chart.items()} def feasible_masses(masses, target, tol, scale=MASS_SCALE): lo, hi = round(scale * (target - tol)), round(scale * (target + tol)) l = bisect.bisect_right(masses, lo); r = bisect.bisect_left(masses, hi) return set(masses[l:r].tolist()) ``` ::: Now run both decoders and count how many candidate expansions each filter kills: ::: {#cell-fig-pruning .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Run knapsack beam search and compare pruning"} completed_k, snap_k, prune_knap = beam_decode(use_knapsack=True) m = prune_plain[["step", "mass_pruned"]].merge( prune_knap[["step", "knapsack_pruned"]], on="step") fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3)) ax.bar(m.step - 0.2, m.mass_pruned, width=0.4, color="#0b5e54", label="mass filter") ax.bar(m.step + 0.2, m.knapsack_pruned, width=0.4, color="#c0392b", label="knapsack (extra)") ax.set_xlabel("decoding step"); ax.set_ylabel("residues pruned"); ax.legend() fig.tight_layout(); fig.savefig("pruning.png", dpi=130) print(f"mass filter pruned {int(m.mass_pruned.sum())} expansions; " f"knapsack pruned {int(m.knapsack_pruned.sum())} more.") print("Knapsack top-2:", [("".join(c["seq"]), round(c["logp"], 2)) for c in completed_k[:2]]) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` mass filter pruned 88 expansions; knapsack pruned 161 more. Knapsack top-2: [('PEPTLDE', np.float64(-1.91)), ('PEPTIDE', np.float64(-1.91))] ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Residues pruned per decoding step. The mass filter only bites at the very end (when residues finally overshoot); the knapsack prunes doomed branches several steps earlier, keeping the beam full of viable hypotheses.](index_files/figure-html/fig-pruning-output-2.png){#fig-pruning width=662 height=278} ::: ::: Same answer (PEPTLDE / PEPTIDE), but the knapsack reaches it having explored far fewer dead ends, the red bars show it discarding doomed branches mid-decode, exactly where the mass filter is still silent. On real spectra, with longer peptides and a vocabulary full of modifications, that pruning is the difference between a beam packed with viable candidates and one clogged with branches that can never balance. ## When it's worth it The chart is the cost: a DFS up to `MAX_MASS = 4000 Da` at 0.1 mDa resolution, built **once** and reused for every spectrum (InstaNovo can pickle it to disk and reload). After that, each filter step is a binary search plus a boolean lookup. So knapsack beam search is the default when you're decoding many spectra against a fixed residue set; plain mass-constrained beam search is the leaner choice for one-off runs or when the residue vocabulary keeps changing. ## Plugging in a real model Everything above swapped one function, `score_next`, for a toy. The real contract is the [`Decodable`](https://github.com/instadeepai/InstaNovo/blob/55a9a3ff7c18e2748c0ef7fc6ba3213002ad6a0f/instanovo/inference/interfaces.py) interface: give the decoder a model that can encode a spectrum and score the next residue, and the same mass-constrained search runs on top of it. The production versions are batched over beams and spectra and run on GPU, but the skeleton is the one you just watched: - [`beam_search.py`](https://github.com/instadeepai/InstaNovo/blob/55a9a3ff7c18e2748c0ef7fc6ba3213002ad6a0f/instanovo/inference/beam_search.py), mass-constrained beam search - [`knapsack.py`](https://github.com/instadeepai/InstaNovo/blob/55a9a3ff7c18e2748c0ef7fc6ba3213002ad6a0f/instanovo/inference/knapsack.py), the chart and its DFS construction - [`knapsack_beam_search.py`](https://github.com/instadeepai/InstaNovo/blob/55a9a3ff7c18e2748c0ef7fc6ba3213002ad6a0f/instanovo/inference/knapsack_beam_search.py), the two combined > *The embedded code is a teaching reimplementation of InstaNovo's decoding logic > (Apache-2.0), reduced to single-spectrum, CPU, pure-Python form. Pinned to commit > `55a9a3f`.*
